John Van Sickle, 'The Bookroll and Some Conventions of the Poetic
Book,' Arethusa 13 (1980) 5-42.
Alpers' version of the pastoral poems continues to win supporters. Van
Sickle has adopted a combative position vis-á-vis unfavourable criticism and
has challenged DuQuesnay's reflective critique inthe Liverpool monthly. Van
Sickle's argument, that the Eclogues form an integrated design, together with
his arguments regarding the composition and arrangement of Roman poetry
books, attains a high-water mark inVergilian criticism.

Anderson underscores the interaction of generic aspects as a novelty in
epic composition. He detects happy (comical)
notes in the Dido episode
which heighten the resonance and emotional impact of the pathetic aspects.
Galinsky examines the
main
currents of recent criticism
of the epic. His
concern is with Vergil's adaptation of Achilles, Odysseus, and Hercules in
Aeneas.
Harrison's compact study of the structure of the epic is required
reading.
Lowell's contribution,
however meagre, notes that Aeneas 'exemplifies
the grit,torture, and sacrifice that made Rome's unification of the
Mediterranean possible. Aeneas puzzles ...
(he is) a force more than a person.' Primmer analyses Books 7-12 as hórrida bella (Aen. 7.41). Segal's article
studies the epic journey as passage into the art of the past, a journey into the
artist's past. The Tiber passage inAen.
8 is progression back to Arcadian
purity, to Arcadian vision, and the aspiration of youth, a return to origins.
There is a tension between the Orphic and Aristean visions of life and art.
Violence, pain, and sacrifice are complements to significant action (city foundation, epic composition). There is a characteristic tension throughout between
aesthetic
distances and emotional
involvements. Wlosok
treats
the
pater familias figure, a parallel study to Owen Lee's recent work (#47).

which was associated with the Sun god prior to Aeneas.

Poehlmann argues that the Hadrumetum (Sousse) mosaic derives from an
Augustan original. The juxtaposition of the poet with Clio and Melpomene
reflects Hellenistic and Imperial theories of poetry's relationship to history
and tragedy. Rawson's article suggests that Q. Cornificius, involved with the
Catullian circle, may have been a friend of Vergil who would have favoured
his De Etymis Deorum.

W. Zurowska-Gorecka, 'De vetustis glossis polonicis ingnesnensibus
schedis vergilianis adhibitis,' (Polish, Latin abstract) SPhP 3 (1977) 61-72.
Bolz examines a manuscript of the Georgics at Gniezno, supplies a text
and an abbreviated commentary, along with a close study of the final verses
of Geo. 1. Krochalis explores a recently acquired partial Aeneid manuscript at
Princeton's Firestone Library. It is the only extant Vergil ms. written not in
book form but on a roll of unusual dimensions: 2!4 inches across, 8V2feet
long! The ms. (ca. 1400-1425) is the work of an Italian scribe experimenting
with a new script (there are Gothic survivals) and an old format.

Harrison reviews Brant's Latin poetry and developing use of Vergil. Leach
examines Panofsky's interpretation of Vergil's Arcadia inthe Eclogues. The
Arcadia of Ed. 10.31-34 is nota reflection of the whole world of the poems.
Jacopo Sannazaro provided a more vivid topography during the Renaissance.
Murrin adduces impressive evidence in his chapter on Landino's Vergil commentary to support the view that the Aeneid adopts the philosophical position of Academic scepticism
(cf. Cicero, Académica, De Natura Deorum,
Tusculan Disputations).
Peri explores echoes of Vergil in Byzantine works;
Virgilius appears to have been a rather common name during the era. Rutledge studies Emma Bovary, Milly Theale and Blanche DuBois inconnection
with Vergil's tragic queen. Affinity to Dido gives them personal magnitude
and
tragic greatness. Wordsworth
frames 'Laodamia' within his reading of
Aeneid 6; he also provides a translation of most of Aeneid 1-3.

Gayle Edward Wilson, 'A "Noble" Comparison in Dryden's "Heroic
Stanzas",' CB 57 (1981) 71-3.
Harrison reviews Brant's Latin poetry and developing use of Vergil. Leach
examines Panofsky's interpretation of Vergil's Arcadia inthe Eclogues. The
Arcadia of Ed. 10.31-34 is nota reflection of the whole world of the poems.
Jacopo Sannazaro provided a more vivid topography during the Renaissance.
Murrin adduces impressive evidence in his chapter on Landino's Vergil com-
mentary to support the view that the Aeneid adopts the philosophical posi-
tion of Academic scepticism
(cf. Cicero, Académica, De Natura Deorum,
Tusculan Disputations).
Peri explores echoes of Vergil in Byzantine works;
Virgilius appears to have been a rather common name during the era. Rut-
ledge studies Emma Bovary, Milly Theale and Blanche DuBois inconnection
with Vergil's tragic queen. Affinity to Dido gives them personal magnitude
and
tragic greatness. Wordsworth
frames 'Laodamia' within his reading of
Aeneid 6; he also provides a translation of most of Aeneid 1-3.
McMaster University
August, 1981
A. G. McKay
